topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting unease. Sarah, my manager, leaned back in her chair, a practiced, placid smile fixed on her face. "I think it’s time you took the lead on onboarding Mark," she announced, her tone suggesting this was a natural progression, not a sudden, unwelcome shift. My hands, resting on the edge of my desk, tightened until my knuckles were white. I managed a tight nod, the movement feeling stiff and unnatural. Inside, a cold knot began to form in my stomach, a heavy, unwelcome presence that made breathing shallow. I wanted to articulate the sudden clench in my chest, the way my jaw felt locked, but the words caught, snagged on the sudden, sharp awareness of being sidelined.

[story 2]

I watched the documents pile up on my desk, the sheer volume of tasks suddenly feeling insurmountable. When David called me into his office, the air immediately felt thin. "I need you to shadow Mark for the next month," he said, his voice clipped and devoid of warmth. I kept my gaze fixed on the polished wood of his desk, focusing intently on the grain, trying to keep my expression neutral. A tremor ran through my left hand as I reached for my water glass, the condensation making my grip unsteady. Every suggestion he made about the transition felt like a deliberate dismissal of the work I had built up, a quiet erasure of my contribution. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the action feel laborious.

[story 3]

The afternoon sun slanted across the floor, illuminating dust motes dancing in the air. When the directive came—that I was to mentor the new hire—a strange hollowness settled over me. I remember nodding along, a performance of compliance. Later, walking past the new employee’s workstation, I felt a distinct pulling sensation in my chest, like a string being gently but firmly tugged. I offered a brief, professional greeting, my voice sounding strangely flat, almost mechanical. I kept my shoulders slightly hunched, a protective gesture against an invisible chill. As I turned to leave, I could feel the weight of unspoken disappointment pressing down, heavy and suffocating.
